question_text: The Profit and Loss Appropriation Account is prepared to:
explanation: The P&L Appropriation Account is prepared AFTER the main P&L Account. It shows how the net profit is distributed (appropriated) among partners — through interest on capital, salary, commission, and the remaining profit in the profit-sharing ratio. It is distinct from the main P&L Account which records business incomes and expenses.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Record business expenses of the firm
Calculate the net profit of the firm
Distribute net profit among partners as per the deed
Record the personal transactions of partners

elimination_hints.hint: Business expenses are recorded in the main P&L Account, not the Appropriation Account. The Appropriation Account deals with profit distribution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the main P&L Account with the P&L Appropriation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: Net profit is calculated in the main P&L Account (Trading A/c → P&L A/c). The Appropriation Account starts with the net profit already calculated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the Appropriation Account calculates profit instead of distributing it.

elimination_hints.hint: Personal transactions are recorded in partners' Capital/Current Accounts, not in the Appropriation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the purpose of Appropriation Account with individual partner accounts.

question_text: Which of the following appears on the credit side of the Profit and Loss Appropriation Account?
explanation: The credit side of P&L Appropriation Account shows: (1) Net Profit transferred from P&L Account, and (2) Interest on drawings charged to partners. The debit side shows: Interest on capital, partner's salary, commission, and the remaining profit distributed in PSR. Interest on drawings increases the profit available for distribution, hence it is credited.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Interest on capital is an appropriation (distribution) of profit, so it appears on the DEBIT side of the Appropriation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing debit-side items with credit-side items in the Appropriation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: Partner's salary is an appropriation — it reduces the distributable profit and appears on the DEBIT side.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the layout of the P&L Appropriation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: Commission, like salary, is debited to the Appropriation Account as it is a distribution of profit to the partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating commission as an income of the firm rather than an expense against profits.

question_text: A and B are partners with capitals of ₹4,00,000 and ₹3,00,000. They share profits in 3:2. Interest on capital is 10% p.a. A's salary is ₹5,000/month, B's commission is 5% on net profit after all charges. Net profit is ₹2,50,000. Interest on B's loan of ₹1,00,000 at 8% is a charge. The distributable profit is:
explanation:
Step 1: Interest on B's loan (charge) = 1,00,000 × 8% = ₹8,000. This is already deducted from net profit. So net profit after charge = ₹2,50,000 (given as net profit, meaning after charges).
Step 2: Interest on capital: A = 4,00,000 × 10% = ₹40,000. B = 3,00,000 × 10% = ₹30,000. Total = ₹70,000.
Step 3: A's salary = 5,000 × 12 = ₹60,000.
Step 4: B's commission = 5% of net profit after all charges. If net profit = ₹2,50,000, commission = 5% × 2,50,000 = ₹12,500.
Step 5: Distributable profit = 2,50,000 − 70,000 − 60,000 − 12,500 = ₹1,07,500.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,80,000 = 2,50,000 − 70,000. This deducts only interest on capital, forgetting A's salary (₹60,000) and B's commission (₹12,500).
elimination_hints.misconception: Only deducting interest on capital and ignoring salary and commission.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,20,000 = 2,50,000 − 70,000 − 60,000. This deducts interest on capital and salary but misses B's commission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to deduct the commission before arriving at distributable profit.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,42,500 might result from not deducting B's commission correctly or miscalculating one of the appropriations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in one of the deduction steps.

question_text: In a partnership, if the firm incurs a net loss, which of the following is correct?
explanation: When the firm incurs a net loss: (1) No interest on capital is allowed (unless the deed specifically states it is payable even in case of loss). (2) Partner's salary and commission (if appropriations) are also not payable from losses — they are payable only out of profits. (3) Interest on partner's loan is still payable because it is a CHARGE against profits (business expense), not an appropriation. (4) The net loss is shared among partners in their profit-sharing ratio (PSR).
subtopic: Treatment in case of net loss
subject: accountancy

options.text: Interest on partner's loan is still payable as it is a charge
Interest on capital must still be paid to all partners
Partners' salary is payable even if the firm incurs a loss
No interest is payable on partner's loan during a loss year

elimination_hints.hint: Interest on capital is an appropriation of profit. If there is no profit, there is nothing to appropriate — unless the deed explicitly says interest is a charge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between charge (always payable) and appropriation (only from profits).

elimination_hints.hint: Salary as an appropriation is payable only out of profits. In a loss year, no salary is given unless the deed treats it as a charge against profits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating salary as a guaranteed payment regardless of the firm's profitability.

elimination_hints.hint: Interest on partner's loan is a business obligation (charge), similar to interest on any other loan. It must be paid regardless of profit or loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing loan interest (charge — always payable) with capital interest (appropriation — only from profits).

question_text: H and G are partners sharing profits in 3:1. H drew ₹15,000 in the middle of every quarter. Interest on drawings is 12% p.a. The interest on drawings charged to H is:
explanation: When drawings are in the middle of each quarter, the average period for interest calculation = (10.5 + 7.5 + 4.5 + 1.5) / 4 = 24/4 = 6 months. Alternatively, for drawings in the middle of equal periods, average period = 6 months always. Total drawings = 15,000 × 4 = ₹60,000. Interest on drawings = 60,000 × 12/100 × 6/12 = 60,000 × 0.06 = ₹3,600.
subtopic: Interest on drawings — middle of quarter
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,500 = 60,000 × 12% × 7.5/12. This uses 7.5 months (beginning of quarter average period) instead of 6 months (middle of quarter).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the beginning-of-quarter average period instead of middle-of-quarter.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,700 = 60,000 × 12% × 4.5/12. This uses 4.5 months (end of quarter) instead of 6 months (middle of quarter).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the end-of-quarter average period instead of middle-of-quarter.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹7,200 = 60,000 × 12%. This charges interest for the full 12 months without using the average period method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying annual interest rate to total drawings without using the average period.

question_text: W, X, Y and Z are partners sharing profits in 4:3:2:1. W and X guarantee Y's share to be not less than ₹75,000 (guarantee borne by W and X in 3:2). Z has guaranteed the firm a minimum profit of ₹4,00,000. Actual profit is ₹3,60,000. What is the total deficiency borne by Z?
explanation:
Z guaranteed the firm ₹4,00,000, but actual profit is only ₹3,60,000. Deficiency by Z = 4,00,000 − 3,60,000 = ₹40,000. Z must pay this ₹40,000 to the firm. After Z's contribution, total distributable = 3,60,000 + 40,000 = ₹4,00,000.
Now check Y's guarantee: Y's share = 4,00,000 × 2/10 = ₹80,000. Y's guaranteed minimum = ₹75,000. Since ₹80,000 > ₹75,000, no deficiency for Y. So Z's total deficiency = ₹40,000 only.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹36,000 = Z's actual share (3,60,000 × 1/10). But the question asks about Z's deficiency as guarantor to the firm, not Z's profit share. Deficiency = 4,00,000 − 3,60,000 = ₹40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Z's share of profit with Z's guarantee obligation.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹75,000 is Y's guaranteed minimum amount, which is an entirely separate guarantee. Z's obligation is the firm-level guarantee of ₹4,00,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up Y's guaranteed amount with Z's guarantee to the firm.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,00,000 is the guaranteed profit level, not the deficiency. Z only bears the shortfall: 4,00,000 − 3,60,000 = ₹40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating the entire guaranteed amount as the deficiency instead of only the shortfall.

question_text: Which of the following items is NOT debited to the Profit and Loss Appropriation Account?
explanation: Manager's salary is a business expense (charge against profit) — it is debited to the main Profit and Loss Account, not the Appropriation Account. Only items that represent distribution of profit among PARTNERS (interest on capital, partner's salary, partner's commission, and profit shares) are debited to the P&L Appropriation Account. The distinction is: charges go to P&L A/c, appropriations go to P&L Appropriation A/c.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Interest on capital is an appropriation of profit credited to partners. It is DEBITED to the Appropriation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing interest on capital as an appropriation item.

elimination_hints.hint: Partner's salary is a profit appropriation, debited to the P&L Appropriation Account. It is NOT a business expense.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating partner's salary the same as employee salary.

elimination_hints.hint: Partner's commission is also an appropriation of profit, debited to the P&L Appropriation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between partner's commission and non-partner expenses.

question_text: P, Q and R are partners sharing profits in 5:3:2. P's capital is ₹5,00,000, Q's is ₹3,00,000 and R's is ₹2,00,000. Interest on capital @ 10% p.a. P's salary ₹10,000/month. Q's commission is 10% on net profit after charging commission. R is guaranteed ₹80,000 by the firm. Net profit = ₹3,00,000. What is Q's total income from the firm?
explanation:
Step 1: Interest on capital — P: ₹50,000, Q: ₹30,000, R: ₹20,000. Total = ₹1,00,000.
Step 2: P's salary = 10,000 × 12 = ₹1,20,000.
Step 3: Q's commission = 10/110 × 3,00,000 = ₹27,273 (10% on profit AFTER charging commission).
Step 4: Distributable profit = 3,00,000 − 1,00,000 − 1,20,000 − 27,273 = ₹52,727.
Step 5: Shares — P: 52,727 × 5/10 = ₹26,364. Q: 52,727 × 3/10 = ₹15,818. R: 52,727 × 2/10 = ₹10,545.
Step 6: R's total = 20,000 + 10,545 = ₹30,545. Guaranteed = ₹80,000. Deficiency = ₹49,455. Borne by P and Q in 5:3. P bears = 49,455 × 5/8 = ₹30,909. Q bears = 49,455 × 3/8 = ₹18,546.
Step 7: Q's total = Interest ₹30,000 + Commission ₹27,273 + Profit share ₹15,818 − Guarantee deficiency ₹18,546 = ₹54,545.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹73,091 = 30,000 + 27,273 + 15,818. This adds interest, commission and profit share but forgets to deduct Q's share of guarantee deficiency (₹18,546).
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that guarantee deficiency reduces the guaranteeing partners' income.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹45,818 = 30,000 + 15,818. This includes only interest and profit share, missing Q's commission (₹27,273) and not adjusting for guarantee deficiency.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting commission and guarantee adjustment in the total computation.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 is only Q's interest on capital. Q also earns commission, profit share, minus the guarantee deficiency obligation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only one component of Q's total income.

question_text: A partner made the following drawings during the year: ₹20,000 on 1st April, ₹30,000 on 1st August, ₹10,000 on 1st December. If interest on drawings is 9% p.a., the interest on drawings for the year ending 31st March is:
explanation:
For unequal drawings at different dates, interest is calculated on each drawing separately:
1st April (12 months to 31st March): 20,000 × 9% × 12/12 = ₹1,800.
1st August (8 months to 31st March): 30,000 × 9% × 8/12 = ₹1,800.
1st December (4 months to 31st March): 10,000 × 9% × 4/12 = ₹300.
Total interest on drawings = 1,800 + 1,800 + 300 = ₹3,900.
subtopic: Interest on drawings — unequal at different dates
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,400 = 60,000 × 9% = total drawings × rate for full year. This ignores the different dates and charges interest for 12 months on the total.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying full-year interest to the total drawings without considering individual dates.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,700 = 60,000 × 9% × 6/12. This uses an average of 6 months, which only works for equal monthly drawings, not unequal amounts on specific dates.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the average period method when drawings are unequal and on specific dates.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,050 results from a calculation error. Verify each product: 1,800 + 1,800 + 300 = 3,900.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in one of the three interest calculations.

question_text: The following information relates to a firm: Total assets ₹15,00,000; External liabilities ₹3,00,000; Non-trade investments ₹2,00,000; Average profit ₹1,80,000 (including ₹20,000 income from non-trade investments). Normal rate of return is 10%. Goodwill by capitalisation of super profit method is:
explanation:
Step 1: Capital Employed = Total Assets − External Liabilities − Non-trade Investments = 15,00,000 − 3,00,000 − 2,00,000 = ₹10,00,000.
(Non-trade investments are excluded because their income is not from the core business.)
Step 2: Adjusted Average Profit = 1,80,000 − 20,000 (non-trade income) = ₹1,60,000.
Step 3: Normal Profit = 10,00,000 × 10% = ₹1,00,000.
Step 4: Super Profit = 1,60,000 − 1,00,000 = ₹60,000.
Step 5: Goodwill = 60,000 × 100/10 = ₹6,00,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,00,000 might result from not deducting non-trade investment income from average profit. Adjusted average profit = 1,80,000 − 20,000 = ₹1,60,000, not ₹1,80,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including non-trade investment income in the business profit for goodwill calculation.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹10,00,000 is the capital employed, not goodwill. Goodwill = Super Profit capitalised = 60,000 × 100/10 = ₹6,00,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital employed with goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,00,000 might result from not excluding non-trade investments from total assets when calculating capital employed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including non-trade investments in capital employed while excluding their income.

question_text: The partnership deed is silent about the method of maintaining capital accounts. In this case, capital accounts are maintained by:
explanation: When the partnership deed does not specify the method of maintaining capital accounts, the default is the FLUCTUATING capital method. Under this method, only one account (Capital Account) is maintained for each partner, and all items (interest on capital, salary, commission, drawings, interest on drawings, share of profit/loss) are recorded in this account. The fixed capital method requires explicit agreement in the deed.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Any method chosen by the managing partner

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed Capital Method requires an explicit agreement because it involves maintaining two separate accounts (Capital + Current). Without such agreement, the simpler fluctuating method is used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the fixed capital method is the default.

elimination_hints.hint: No single partner has the authority to decide accounting methods unilaterally. In the absence of a deed, default rules apply — fluctuating method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming a managing partner can override default accounting practices.

elimination_hints.hint: Only one method is used at a time — either fixed or fluctuating. Using both simultaneously is not possible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that these are mutually exclusive methods.

question_text: U and V are equal partners. U introduced additional capital of ₹50,000 on 1st July. Interest on capital is allowed at 12% p.a. The interest on additional capital for the year ending 31st March is:
explanation: Additional capital introduced on 1st July. Period from 1st July to 31st March = 9 months. Interest on additional capital = 50,000 × 12/100 × 9/12 = ₹4,500. Note: Interest is calculated only for the period the additional capital was available to the firm.
subtopic: Interest on additional capital for part year
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,000 = 50,000 × 12%. This charges interest for a full 12 months, but the capital was introduced on 1st July — only 9 months remain till 31st March.
elimination_hints.misconception: Charging full-year interest on capital introduced mid-year.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,000 = 50,000 × 12% × 6/12. This calculates for 6 months, but 1st July to 31st March is 9 months, not 6.
elimination_hints.misconception: Miscounting the number of months from July to March.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,000 = 50,000 × 10%. This uses the wrong interest rate (10% instead of 12%).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the wrong interest rate in the calculation.

question_text:
Assertion (A): A partner's drawings reduce the balance in their Capital Account under the fluctuating capital method.
Reason (R): Under the fluctuating capital method, all transactions are recorded in a single Capital Account which keeps changing (fluctuating).
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — drawings are debited to the Capital Account under the fluctuating method, reducing the balance. The Reason is also TRUE — under the fluctuating method, all items (drawings, salary, interest, profit/loss) are recorded in one Capital Account, causing it to constantly change. The Reason correctly explains the Assertion — because everything goes to one account (fluctuating method), drawings reduce the capital balance.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY drawings affect the capital account — because under the fluctuating method, ALL transactions (including drawings) go to the capital account. Clear causal link.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the connection between the single-account structure and drawings reducing capital.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — the fluctuating capital method does use a single account for all transactions, making the balance fluctuate. This is the defining characteristic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the fluctuating method uses multiple accounts.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — drawings are indeed debited to the Capital Account under fluctuating method, reducing the balance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking drawings don't affect the Capital Account under the fluctuating method.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Interest on capital and partner's salary are treated the same way — both are debited to the P&L Appropriation Account.
Reason (R): Both interest on capital and partner's salary are appropriations of profit and not charges against profit.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — both interest on capital and partner's salary are debited to the P&L Appropriation Account (assuming they are treated as appropriations, which is the default treatment). The Reason is also TRUE — both are appropriations (distributions from profit), not charges (business expenses). The Reason correctly explains the Assertion — because both are appropriations, they are debited to the Appropriation Account (not the main P&L Account).
subtopic: Nature of interest on capital and salary
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY both are debited to the Appropriation Account — BECAUSE they are appropriations, not charges. The causal link is explicit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the nature (appropriation) with the accounting treatment (debit to Appropriation A/c).

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — both interest on capital and salary ARE appropriations of profit by default in partnership accounting.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking one of them is a charge while the other is an appropriation.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — both items are debited to the P&L Appropriation Account as per standard partnership accounting treatment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking one is debited to P&L A/c (as charge) while the other to P&L Appropriation A/c.

question_text: Under the fluctuating capital method, there is no need to maintain a separate Current Account for each partner.
explanation: TRUE. Under the fluctuating capital method, only ONE account — the Capital Account — is maintained for each partner. All transactions (drawings, salary, interest on capital, interest on drawings, share of profit/loss, commission, etc.) are recorded directly in this Capital Account. There is no separate Current Account. The Current Account is only needed under the Fixed Capital Method.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Under the fluctuating method, a single Capital Account handles all entries. The Current Account exists ONLY under the Fixed Capital Method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Current Account is always required regardless of the capital method used.

question_text: A minor can be admitted to a partnership firm as a full partner with unlimited liability.
explanation: FALSE. A minor CANNOT be a full partner in a firm. Under Section 30 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932, a minor can only be admitted to the BENEFITS of the partnership with the consent of all partners. A minor's liability is limited to their share of the profits and the property of the firm. A minor does not have unlimited liability and cannot participate in management decisions.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: A minor lacks legal capacity to enter into a contract. Under Section 30, they can only be admitted to the BENEFITS of partnership — not as a full partner with unlimited liability.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that minors have special status under the Partnership Act.

question_text:
Match the following partnership terms with their correct descriptions:

Column I:
(i) Mutual Agency
(ii) Profit & Loss Appropriation Account
(iii) Fixed Capital Method
(iv) Interest on Partner's Loan

Column II:
(P) Each partner is an agent of the firm and principal of other partners
(Q) Two accounts maintained — Capital Account and Current Account
(R) A charge against profit, debited to P&L Account
(S) Shows the distribution of net profit among partners
explanation: (i) Mutual Agency → (P): Each partner acts as agent of the firm and principal of other partners — this is the legal foundation of partnership. (ii) P&L Appropriation Account → (S): It shows how net profit is distributed (interest on capital, salary, commission, remaining profit in PSR). (iii) Fixed Capital Method → (Q): Under this method, two accounts are maintained — a Capital Account (constant) and a Current Account (for all adjustments). (iv) Interest on Partner's Loan → (R): It is a charge against profit (business expense), debited to the main P&L Account.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This matches P&L Appropriation Account (ii) with (Q) — two accounts. But P&L Appropriation shows profit distribution (S). Two accounts (Q) describes the Fixed Capital Method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing P&L Appropriation Account with the Fixed Capital Method.

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Mutual Agency (i) with (S) — profit distribution. Mutual Agency is about the agent-principal relationship (P), not profit distribution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the legal concept of mutual agency with the accounting concept of profit distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Fixed Capital Method (iii) with (R) — charge against profit. Fixed Capital Method is about maintaining two accounts (Q). Charge against profit (R) describes loan interest.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the descriptions of Fixed Capital Method and Interest on Loan.

question_text: The maximum number of partners allowed in a partnership firm as per the Indian Partnership Act is _______.
explanation: As per Rule 10 of the Companies (Miscellaneous) Rules, 2014 (read with Section 464 of the Companies Act, 2013), the maximum number of partners in a firm is 50. For banking business, the limit was previously 10 and for other businesses 20, but these limits were revised to 50 for all types of businesses.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 10 was the old limit for banking business before the revision. The current limit is 50 for all types of businesses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Remembering the old limit for banking business partnerships.

elimination_hints.hint: 20 was the old limit for non-banking businesses. Under the revised rules, the maximum has been increased to 50 for all businesses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old limit of 20 which applied to non-banking firms before revision.

elimination_hints.hint: 100 is not the correct limit. The maximum is 50 partners as per the current rules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing partnership limits with other corporate structures.

question_text: E and F are partners sharing profits in 3:2. Their fixed capitals are ₹4,00,000 and ₹2,00,000. Interest on capital @ 10%. E's salary ₹8,000/month. During 2025-26, E drew ₹10,000 at the end of each month (interest on drawings = ₹6,600). F made a single drawing of ₹50,000 on 1st October (interest = ₹2,500). The firm's net profit (after debiting interest on F's loan of ₹1,00,000 @ 6%) was ₹1,70,000. Prepare the statement showing each partner's share.
explanation:
P&L Appropriation Account:
Credit side: Net Profit ₹1,70,000 + Interest on E's drawings ₹6,600 + Interest on F's drawings ₹2,500 = ₹1,79,100.
Debit side: Interest on capital — E: ₹40,000, F: ₹20,000 = ₹60,000. E's salary = 8,000 × 12 = ₹96,000.
Distributable profit = 1,79,100 − 60,000 − 96,000 = ₹23,100.
E's share = 23,100 × 3/5 = ₹13,860. F's share = 23,100 × 2/5 = ₹9,240.
E's total credit = 40,000 + 96,000 + 13,860 = ₹1,49,860. E's debit = 1,20,000 (drawings) + 6,600 (int on drawings) = ₹1,26,600. Net credit to E's current A/c = ₹23,260.
F's total credit = 20,000 + 9,240 = ₹29,240. F's debit = 50,000 + 2,500 = ₹52,500. Net debit to F's current A/c = ₹23,260.
Distributable profit share: E = ₹13,860, F = ₹9,240.
subject: accountancy

options.text: E: ₹8,460 and F: ₹5,640 as profit share
E: ₹13,860 and F: ₹9,240 as profit share
E: ₹1,02,000 and F: ₹68,000 as profit share
E: ₹7,260 and F: ₹4,840 as profit share

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,460 + ₹5,640 = ₹14,100. This might result from not adding back interest on drawings to the distributable profit. The correct distributable = ₹23,100.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to add interest on drawings to the credit side of P&L Appropriation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,02,000 + ₹68,000 = ₹1,70,000. This distributes the entire net profit in 3:2 without deducting interest on capital and salary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing net profit directly without any appropriations.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹7,260 + ₹4,840 = ₹12,100. This likely deducts interest on drawings instead of adding it, reducing the distributable profit incorrectly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Subtracting interest on drawings from profit instead of adding it.

question_text:
Arrange the following in the correct order as they appear in the P&L Appropriation Account (debit side, top to bottom):
(i) Transfer of remaining profit to partners' capital accounts in PSR
(ii) Partner's commission
(iii) Interest on capital
(iv) Partner's salary
explanation: The standard order on the debit side of P&L Appropriation Account is: (1) Interest on Capital (iii) — first appropriation. (2) Partner's Salary (iv) — second appropriation. (3) Partner's Commission (ii) — third appropriation. (4) Remaining profit transferred to partners in PSR (i) — final distribution. This order follows the convention: fixed entitlements first (interest, salary, commission), then the variable residual profit. Order: (iii) → (iv) → (ii) → (i).
subtopic: Order of items in P&L Appropriation Account
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This places salary (iv) first. The conventional order starts with interest on capital (iii), then salary, then commission, then profit distribution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not following the standard order of appropriations in the Appropriation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: This places profit distribution (i) FIRST. Profit distribution is always the LAST item — only the residual after all appropriations is distributed in PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing profit before making other appropriations.

elimination_hints.hint: This places commission (ii) before salary (iv). The conventional order is: interest on capital → salary → commission → profit share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the order of salary and commission in the Appropriation Account.
